subject: History
chapter: The Making of a Global World

key_points: Globalisation has a long history involving the movement of people, goods, and capital across vast distances.
The Silk Routes are prime examples of early pre-modern trade and cultural links connecting distant parts of Asia with Europe and Northern Africa.
Food traveled too: many of our common foods like potatoes, soya, groundnuts, maize, and chilies only reached Asia after the discovery of the Americas.
The 19th century was shaped by three types of 'flows' within international economic exchanges: Trade, Labour (migration), and Capital (investment).
Technological inventions like railways, steamships, and the telegraph were vital in accelerating global connections in the 19th century.
The late 19th-century colonial expansion led to the 'scramble for Africa' and the forced integration of many societies into the world economy.
examples: Silk Route Goods: Chinese silk and pottery going to Europe; Gold and silver flowing from Europe to Asia.
Food Migration: Noodles traveling from China to become Spaghetti in Italy; Potatoes becoming a staple for Europe's poor.
Indentured Labour: Millions of Indians and Chinese traveling to work on plantations in the Caribbean, Fiji, and Mauritius.
Technological Impact: Refrigerated ships allowing the transport of perishable meat from America to Europe.
formulas: Economic Exchange = Flow of Trade + Flow of Labour + Flow of Capital
Global World = Technological Innovation + Colonial Expansion + Market Integration
Biological Exchange = Discovery of New Lands + Migration of Crops/Diseases
exam_tips: Understand that 'El Dorado' was the fabled city of gold that lured Europeans to South America.
Remember that the Rinderpest (cattle plague) in Africa in the 1890s illustrates how disease aided colonial conquest.
Focus on how the Corn Laws' abolition in Britain led to cheaper food imports and the growth of global agriculture.
Note that the first printed books and textiles were major items in early global trade.
